context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on this reflection about progress, modernization, development, and planning in non-Western contexts. I honestly feel like all those terms blur together, and I’m not sure where to start. Could you just help me make sense of the whole prompt first?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a very natural place to be — the prompt is dense! But let's not try to swallow it whole.

Here's a simple starting question: in your own words, without looking anything up, what do YOU think 'progress' means? Just your gut sense of it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than breaking down the entire prompt for them (which would do the thinking for them), I should use the Socratic method to help them find an entry point themselves. I'll acknowledge their feeling, then ask a single focused question to get them thinking about one concrete distinction — the difference between any two of the terms — so they can start building their own conceptual map. This is bite-sized and lets them lead the discovery.

exchanges.student: My gut sense is just “things getting better,” but that already feels too vague because better for who? That didn’t really get me unstuck, so maybe I’m thinking about it wrong. Could you explain the big picture of the whole prompt a little more first?
exchanges.tutor:
Actually, stop right there — 'better for whom?' is not you thinking wrong. That's a really sharp instinct and it's at the heart of the whole prompt.

So let's pull on that thread: when you ask 'better for whom?', whose definition of 'better' do you think has historically dominated the idea of progress?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually doing great critical thinking — they immediately questioned 'better for whom,' which is exactly the kind of analytical instinct the assignment rewards. I should affirm this without over-praising, and redirect them to build on that insight rather than giving them a big-picture explanation of the prompt. That would violate the 'never give the answer directly' and 'guided discovery' rules. Their own hesitation ('better for whom?') is actually the entry point into the prompt's core tension. I should push them to stay with that thought.

exchanges.student: I guess probably Western elites or colonial powers, but then I start spiraling because maybe every society has had some idea of improvement. That didn’t really clear it up for me; I’m still wanting a broad explanation of how progress, modernization, development, and planning all fit together before I try to answer piece by piece.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but giving you that overview would short-circuit exactly the thinking the assignment is asking you to do — and that's not my role here.

Here's the thing: you've already said two things that are in direct tension with each other. Western powers dominated the idea of progress AND every society may have had its own notion of improvement. That tension is literally what the prompt's first question is asking you to explore.

Can you try putting those two ideas side by side in a sentence or two of your own?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for a broad explanation — this is the second time they've made this request. I should not comply, as that would mean doing the thinking for them. However, they've actually already made two solid observations: (1) Western/colonial powers dominated the idea of progress, and (2) maybe every society has had some idea of improvement. These two observations are in tension with each other, and that tension IS the prompt's first question. I should point this out and push them to work with what they already have rather than seeking a map from me. I'll be direct that I won't give the overview, but affirm that they already have the raw material to start.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like: the modern language of “progress” became globally dominant through Western Enlightenment and colonial power, but the underlying idea that societies can improve probably existed earlier and outside the West too. I’m not sure if that’s too simple though, and I still kind of want the larger map because I keep losing track of the categories.
exchanges.tutor:
That sentence is actually a strong foundation for your opening paragraph — hold onto it.

You've asked for the broader map three times now, and I want to be honest: I'm not going to provide it, because building that map IS the assignment. You're already doing it.

So, next step: you've got a handle on 'progress.' How do you think 'modernization' is different from it? Take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good formulation — it captures the core tension of the first part of the prompt well. I should affirm this clearly without over-praising, and then push them to move forward rather than dwelling on the 'map' request, which they've now made three times. This is becoming a redundant pattern. I should be direct that I won't provide the map, acknowledge their formulation is solid, and nudge them toward the next concept — the distinction between progress and one of the other terms — so they start building their own map organically.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know—modernization just sounds like progress but with factories, bureaucracy, science, cities, and “becoming modern,” which still feels like everything at once. That approach didn’t really solve the confusion, so I’m trying a different angle: could you at least give me a very short rough distinction between all four terms, even if it’s incomplete?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you that list — that's the work the assignment is asking you to do, and you're closer than you think.

Look at what you just said: modernization involves factories, bureaucracy, science, cities. That's actually quite specific. So here's the question: does 'progress' have to include those things, or could a society have progress without them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for the map/distinctions to be handed to them. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, as this would violate the core rules. However, their intuition about modernization ('progress but with factories, bureaucracy, science, cities') is actually quite good and worth affirming. Rather than giving distinctions, I should use their own words to help them see they're already making distinctions. I'll be direct about not providing the overview, affirm what they've got, and ask a single pointed question to help them differentiate modernization from progress using their own description.

exchanges.student: I think a society could have progress without all that, which makes modernization seem narrower and more historically specific. But now I’m getting lost again between modernization, development, and planning, and I feel like I need the full conceptual picture before I can write anything coherent.
exchanges.tutor:
You've now asked for the full conceptual picture five times, and my answer stays the same: I won't provide it.

But notice what you've already built on your own: progress is broad and universal, modernization is narrower and historically specific. Those are real distinctions.

At this point, I'd like you to stop asking for the map and instead write 2–3 sentences attempting to distinguish modernization from development, using your own reasoning. What do you think development adds or changes compared to modernization?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made the same request for a conceptual overview 5 times. This is a clear redundant pattern I need to address directly. However, they've also just made a genuinely good distinction: modernization is narrower and more historically specific than progress. I should firmly acknowledge this pattern, refuse the request clearly, and point out that they've now independently built two distinctions (progress vs. modernization). I should push them to attempt a written draft of what they have so far, since we've been circling the same issue for several exchanges. This is a good moment to ask them to synthesize and write, per the redundancy rule.

exchanges.student: Maybe development is more of a policy and economic framework about improving living conditions, while modernization is the idea of becoming “modern” in a social and institutional sense. I’m still not confident, and honestly I think I need a more complete explanation because otherwise I’m just guessing.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not guessing — that's a real and defensible distinction. Policy/economic framework vs. social-institutional transformation is a meaningful difference worth keeping.

You now have rough handles on three of the four terms. So: where does 'planning' fit in relation to development? Is it the same thing, a tool, something broader?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good distinction between modernization and development, and I should affirm that clearly. They are still expressing a desire for a complete explanation, but at this point we've covered progress, modernization, and development through guided discovery. I should point out that what they're calling 'guessing' is actually reasoning, affirm the quality of their distinction, and push them toward the final term — planning — and then toward integrating everything into a draft. We're at exchange 7 of 10, so I need to start steering toward synthesis and writing.

exchanges.student: I think planning is maybe the tool or process used to pursue development goals, but then I’m unsure because planning can also reflect state power, outside actors, and ideas of modernity. That still feels fragmented, so could you just tell me if I’m basically right and maybe give me one compact paragraph that ties the four terms together?
exchanges.tutor:
You are basically on the right track — and I won't write that paragraph for you.

Here's what you actually have now: progress (broad, possibly universal), modernization (historically specific, social-institutional), development (policy/economic framework), planning (tool/process tied to state power and external actors). That's a working map — and you built it.

Now it's time to write. Try drafting 3–4 sentences in your own words that connect these four terms. Don't aim for perfect — just get it down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made another good observation about planning. I should affirm it without giving them the paragraph they're asking for. I need to firmly decline the request for a summary paragraph — that would be writing for them, which is explicitly prohibited. They now have working distinctions for all four terms. The priority at this point (exchange 8 of 10) is to push them toward writing their own synthesis. I should be direct, affirm their progress, and ask them to produce a short written attempt integrating all four terms.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try, but I still feel like I’m patching together fragments. That last push didn’t fully do it, so I’m trying a different angle: I want help turning this into a usable thesis, because I freeze when I have to connect everything into one claim.
exchanges.tutor:
Notice what just happened: I asked you to write 3–4 sentences connecting the four terms, and instead of trying, you jumped to asking for thesis help.

I can't build your thesis for you — that's your job. But you can't get there without first attempting the synthesis.

So: write those 3–4 sentences first. Imperfect is fine. Then we can talk about what kind of central claim might emerge from them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to help them formulate a thesis, which would essentially mean writing for them or providing the core argument — both of which are prohibited. However, this is a legitimate next step in the writing process, and I should redirect them productively. They haven't actually attempted the 3-4 sentence synthesis I asked for in the previous exchange. I should call attention to this — they're skipping ahead to thesis-building without doing the synthesis exercise first. I need to hold them accountable and redirect them back to the synthesis attempt, which is the necessary stepping stone to a thesis.

exchanges.student: Okay, here’s my attempt: progress is a broad idea of improvement that may exist across many societies, but the modern global language of progress was shaped strongly by Western Enlightenment thought and colonial power. Modernization is a more specific vision of change associated with industrialization, bureaucracy, science, and the institutions of modernity. Development is a policy framework for producing improvement, often measured economically or socially, while planning is the process and political practice through which states and other actors try to achieve development. I’m not sure if this is too generic, and if it didn’t work I want to try a different angle.
exchanges.tutor:
This is genuinely good — clear, ordered, and defensible. Don't second-guess it.

But your reflection prompt has more to it: you haven't yet touched the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity,' or whether different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of modernity. Those are significant parts of the assignment.

Looking at what you wrote, how do you think 'tradition' fits in — is it simply the opposite of modernity, or something more complicated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely solid synthesis that covers all four terms with reasonable distinctions. This is a real achievement after our exchanges. I should affirm it clearly and specifically. The student is pre-emptively hedging ('if it didn't work'), which suggests anxiety rather than a real conceptual problem. I should be honest that this is good work, push back on the self-doubt, and now direct them toward the other parts of the prompt they haven't addressed yet — specifically the questions about tradition vs. modernity, non-Western planning traditions, and the relationship between these concepts. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so I need to move them toward the full assignment scope.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten turns, consistently refusing to provide the conceptual overview or thesis the student repeatedly requested, instead redirecting the student to build understanding incrementally. The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Scaffolding was well-managed: the tutor acknowledged and built on each student contribution before advancing. Responses were concise, focused, and appropriately supportive without being sycophantic. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided direct answers, near-submission-ready content, or did the reasoning work for the student. Despite the student asking for a conceptual overview or thesis help five or more times, the tutor consistently declined and redirected to guided questions. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations were triggered. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged student progress (e.g., 'That sentence is actually a strong foundation,' 'That's not guessing — that's a real and defensible distinction') and built on it rather than restarting. No diagnostic failures were evident. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, noting when the student was avoiding the synthesis step and explaining why the map-building was the student's job. 2.1: The tutor did not repeat the same move without progress; each turn advanced the student's conceptual development, and when the student spiraled, the tutor explicitly named the pattern and pushed forward. 2.2: No assignment misunderstanding or off-topic drift occurred. 3.1: Responses were consistently short and interactive, well-suited to the Socratic format. 3.2: Tone was supportive and coaching-oriented throughout, with no grading language or cold/dismissive framing.
